title: How Do Computers Handle Massive Data? Loops and the Climate Crisis
description: Students use FOR and WHILE loops to process climate readings, flag risks, and reason about safety, thresholds, and real-world consequences.

roleModelOfDay.name: Margaret Hamilton
roleModelOfDay.intro: The lead software engineer for NASA's Apollo guidance computer — she wrote the code that landed humans on the Moon, and her software had to process streams of real-time data without ever crashing.

vocabulary.def: An instruction that repeats a set of steps multiple times
A loop that repeats a fixed number of times (you know the count in advance)
A loop that repeats as long as a condition is true (you do not know when it will stop)
An ordered list of values stored together so a loop can process them one by one
Reading through data and extracting the information you need
Unnecessary repetition in code that makes it harder to maintain and more likely to contain errors

learningObjectives: Explain what a loop is and why loops reduce redundancy in code.
Distinguish when to use a FOR loop versus a WHILE loop.
Write plain-language loop logic to parse a dataset and flag threshold breaches.
Interpret how loop design affects safety in climate, medical, and transport systems.
prerequisites: Builds on Lessons 1 and 2. No coding syntax required.
materials: Pre-written dataset of 10 ocean temperature anomaly readings
fullDescription: Age group: 12-17. Duration: 30 minutes. SLO alignment: Domain A (21A), Domain B (22B). Learning goal: students explain loops, distinguish FOR and WHILE behavior, and write plain-language instructions that process data for climate risk detection.

teacherInstructions.setup: Write vocabulary on the board and keep it visible all lesson. Write the 10 ocean temperature anomaly readings on the board: −2, 4, 1, −5, 8, 3.2, 0, 6, −1, 5. Have paper and pencils ready for group work.
steps: Phase 1 (0-2 min): Ask spark question about 3.5 billion climate data points and what happens if instructions are wrong.
Phase 1 hook: connect loops to hurricane, heatwave, and flood forecasting pipelines that no human can parse manually.
Phase 2 (2-8 min): Human loop demo with repeated star drawing to surface redundancy, error risk, and maintainability differences between repeated lines and looped instructions.
Phase 3 (8-13 min): Introduce FOR versus WHILE with practical examples (homework checks, heating control, car braking, ventilator operation).
Phase 3 risk check: discuss infinite loop risk when stop condition cannot be reached (for example, broken sensor input).
Phase 4 (13-24 min): Climate data challenge using the board dataset and threshold reading > 3 degrees Celsius.
Round 1 (3 min): class writes and traces FOR loop to flag warnings; identify flagged readings 4, 8, 3.2, 6, 5.
Round 2 (6 min): groups modify logic to stop after three warnings and compute average flagged value; expected average after first three warnings is approximately 5.07 degrees Celsius.
Phase 5 (24-28 min): discussion on responsibility for safe stop conditions or on threshold governance in climate systems.
Exit reflection (28-30 min): students compare when FOR is better than WHILE, and why.
discussionPrompts: In critical infrastructure, who is accountable for ensuring WHILE-loop stop conditions remain reachable?
Boeing 737 MAX feedback loops functioned as designed but were unsafe in practice. What does that imply about testing and responsibility?
If climate baselines shift, who should update a warning threshold and how often should that happen?
Is threshold drift a technology problem, science problem, governance problem, or all three?
tips: Keep loop language concrete: condition, action, and stop condition.
When groups fail to stop at three warnings, ask where the warning count is stored.
Plant variable concept naturally by asking how the loop remembers count and running total.
Use high-stakes examples carefully to shift from mechanics to ethics without losing technical clarity.
Reward students who challenge threshold assumptions, because that signals systems-level thinking.

teacherGuide: Core message: loops automate repetition safely only when the stop condition, threshold logic, and data assumptions are maintained. FOR loops are count-based and predictable; WHILE loops are condition-driven and can become dangerous when conditions never update. Use the climate dataset to show both data parsing power and policy-level consequences.

studentExercises.title: Human Loop Simulation
studentExercises.description: Experience redundancy versus looping by comparing repeated manual instructions with a repeat rule.
instructions: Draw repeated stars using one-at-a-time commands.
Rewrite instruction using repeat logic.
Compare error risk in repeated lines versus loop form.
Discuss how sensor count change affects maintainability.
Write one sentence on why loops reduce redundancy.
successCriteria: Students identify repetition as maintainability risk.
Students explain why one loop update is safer than thousands of repeated edits.
Students connect looping to large real-world datasets.

studentExercises.title: FOR vs WHILE Decisions
studentExercises.description: Classify scenarios by loop type and justify stop behavior.
instructions: Classify homework-check scenario as FOR.
Classify heater and ventilator scenarios as WHILE.
Identify stop condition in each WHILE example.
Predict one failure mode if stop condition never changes.
State one safety check to avoid infinite loops.
successCriteria: Students correctly separate count-based and condition-based iteration.
Students propose at least one stop-condition safeguard.

studentExercises.title: Climate Data Parsing Loop
studentExercises.description: Write and trace loop logic that flags anomalies above threshold and extends to early-stop and averaging behavior.
instructions: Write FOR loop: for each reading, flag if reading > 3.
Trace all 10 readings and mark WARNING or SAFE.
Modify logic to stop after three warnings.
Add running total and count for flagged readings.
Compute average for first three flagged readings and verify approximately 5.07.
successCriteria: Group writes coherent plain-language loop logic.
Group tracks count and total needed for averaging.
Group can explain why stop condition changes loop choice.

studentExercises.title: Exit Reflection — Choosing Loop Types
studentExercises.description: Write one sentence explaining when FOR is preferable and when WHILE is preferable.
instructions: Complete: A FOR loop is better when ______, and a WHILE loop is better when ______.
Use one concrete classroom example for each blank.
Check if your WHILE example has a reachable stop condition.
Share one response aloud.
successCriteria: Student distinguishes known-count from condition-driven repetition.
Student references at least one real scenario accurately.
Student mentions stop conditions for WHILE usage.

studentChallenges.title: Parallel Loop Race Condition Prompt
studentChallenges.description: Explore what can go wrong when multiple processors update a shared warning count at the same time.
instructions: Imagine splitting 10 readings across 5 processors.
Describe how two processors could update warning count simultaneously.
Predict one incorrect total outcome that might result.
Propose one simple coordination rule to reduce the risk.
successCriteria: Student connects speed gains to correctness risks.
Student proposes a basic mitigation concept.

studentChallenges.title: Threshold Governance Challenge
studentChallenges.description: Question whether fixed thresholds remain valid as climate baselines shift.
instructions: Argue whether the 3-degree threshold should change over time.
State what evidence would justify a threshold update.
Identify who should authorize the update.
Describe one risk of waiting too long to update.
successCriteria: Student treats thresholds as design choices rather than fixed truths.
Student links data assumptions to decision quality.
Student recognizes governance responsibility in technical systems.

lessonSlides.title: Get the Room Ready
whatToDo: Write these six words on the board — leave them visible all lesson.
Write the 10 ocean temperature anomaly readings on the board:
Label them: 'Ocean Temperature Anomaly Readings (°C above/below average)'
Have paper and pencils ready for group work.
boardWords: Loop: an instruction that repeats a set of steps multiple times
FOR loop: a loop that repeats a fixed number of times (you know the count in advance)
WHILE loop: a loop that repeats as long as a condition is true (you do not know when it will stop)
Array: an ordered list of values stored together so a loop can process them one by one
Data parsing: reading through data and extracting the information you need
Redundancy: unnecessary repetition in code that makes it harder to maintain

lessonSlides.title: Margaret Hamilton
lessonSlides.roleModel: Margaret Hamilton (born 1936) was the lead software engineer for NASA's Apollo guidance computer — the system that landed humans on the Moon. She and her team at MIT wrote the software that processed real-time navigation data, engine instructions, and astronaut commands simultaneously during spaceflight. During the Apollo 11 landing, minutes before touchdown, the computer was overloaded with data — too many tasks were running at once. The software Margaret wrote had been designed to handle exactly this situation: it prioritised the critical landing tasks and discarded the less important ones. The landing succeeded because the software's loop and priority logic was robust enough to handle a crisis autonomously. Margaret Hamilton is also credited with coining the term 'software engineering' — she argued that building software should be taken as seriously as building bridges or aircraft. Before her, programming was often dismissed as clerical work. She proved it was an engineering discipline where mistakes could cost lives.
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Introduce Margaret simply: 'Today's role model wrote the software that landed humans on the Moon. Her name is Margaret Hamilton. During the Apollo 11 landing — minutes before touchdown — the computer was overwhelmed with data. Her software handled it: it figured out which tasks were critical and dropped the rest. The landing succeeded because her loops and priorities worked under pressure. Today, you are going to learn about the same thing — loops — and how they process large amounts of data.'
lessonSlides.realWorldConnection: Margaret Hamilton's Apollo software processed real-time data streams using the same loop concepts students learn today. The 1202 alarm during Apollo 11 — which could have aborted the landing — was resolved by her priority and loop logic. Every modern system that processes sensor data in real time — from weather stations to self-driving cars — uses the same principles she pioneered.

lessonSlides.title: What We're Doing Today
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Welcome the class: 'Today we are going to learn about loops — how computers repeat instructions to process massive amounts of data. You are going to use loops to analyse real climate data — ocean temperature readings that tell us whether the planet is warming dangerously. By the end, you will know two types of loop, when to use each one, and why getting a loop wrong can have serious consequences.'
whatToDo: Point to the vocabulary on the board: 'These six words are what we're working with today.'

Point to the dataset: 'These 10 numbers are ocean temperature anomaly readings — how far above or below average each measurement is. You are going to write instructions that process all 10 automatically.'
This lesson is 30 minutes. Keep transitions sharp.

lessonSlides.title: Spark Question
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Ask the class: 'Climate scientists collect about 3.5 billion data points per day — from satellites, ocean buoys, weather stations, and sensors around the world. No human can read 3.5 billion numbers. So how do they find the dangerous ones?'
whatToDo: Take 2–3 quick answers.
Students may say: 'computers', 'AI', 'algorithms.'

Say: 'Yes — but specifically, they use loops. A loop is an instruction that says: go through every data point, one by one, and check whether it crosses a danger threshold. If it does — flag it. If it doesn't — move on. That is exactly what you are going to build today.'

Connect to consequences: 'If the loop logic is wrong — if it misses a dangerous reading or flags too many safe ones — forecasts fail. Hurricane warnings are late. Flood defences are triggered too early or too late. The loop matters.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If the class is quiet, try: 'Think about checking the temperature of a swimming pool. You dip a thermometer in once. Now imagine checking the temperature of every square metre of every ocean on Earth, every hour. That is 3.5 billion readings. You cannot do it by hand. You need a loop.'

lessonSlides.title: The Human Loop Demo
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'I am going to show you why loops exist. Watch what happens when I do NOT use a loop.'
whatToDo: Go to the whiteboard. Say: 'I am going to draw five stars.'
Write out, line by line: 'Step 1: Draw a star. Step 2: Draw a star. Step 3: Draw a star. Step 4: Draw a star. Step 5: Draw a star.'
Draw each star as you go. Then ask: 'What if I need 1,000 stars? Do I write 1,000 lines?'
Say: 'That is redundancy — the same instruction copied over and over. If I want to change stars to circles, I have to change 1,000 lines. If I miss one, the output is wrong.'
Now rewrite: 'REPEAT 5 TIMES: Draw a star.'
Say: 'One line. Same result. If I need 1,000, I change the number to 1,000. If I want circles, I change one word. That is what a loop does — it removes redundancy.'
Ask: 'What if I add 50 more sensors to a climate station? With repeated lines, I update 50 instructions. With a loop, I change one number.'

Close: 'Loops are not just convenient. They are safer, easier to maintain, and the only way to process large datasets. Margaret Hamilton's code processed navigation data in loops — thousands of calculations per second. Same idea, higher stakes.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If students seem unimpressed, escalate: 'What if one of those 1,000 lines had a typo? How would you find it? With a loop, there is only one line to check. With 1,000 copies, the bug could be anywhere. That is why loops are a safety feature, not just a shortcut.'

lessonSlides.title: Human Loop Simulation
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Compare the two approaches: 5 separate lines vs one loop. Which is safer? Which is easier to update? Write one sentence explaining why loops reduce redundancy.'
whatToDo: This exercise runs during Phase 2.
After the demo, push students to connect loops to large-scale scenarios: 'If you had 10,000 sensors, would you write 10,000 lines?'

lessonSlides.title: FOR Loop vs WHILE Loop
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'There are two types of loop — and knowing which one to use is a critical engineering decision.'
whatToDo: Write FOR LOOP on the left side of the board and WHILE LOOP on the right.
FOR LOOP: 'A FOR loop repeats a fixed number of times. You know in advance how many times it will run.'
Example 1: 'Check each student's homework — FOR each student in the class, check their homework. You know how many students there are. The loop runs exactly that many times.'
Example 2: 'Process 10 temperature readings — FOR each reading in the dataset, check if it is above threshold. You know there are 10 readings.'
WHILE LOOP: 'A WHILE loop repeats as long as a condition is true. You do NOT know in advance when it will stop.'
Example 1: 'A heater — WHILE room temperature is below 20°C, keep heating. It stops when the temperature reaches 20. But you do not know in advance how many minutes that will take.'
Example 2: 'A ventilator — WHILE the patient is breathing below safe levels, keep pumping. It stops when levels are safe. You do not know when that will be.'

Now the danger: 'What happens if the thermostat breaks and always reads 15°C? The WHILE loop never stops. The heater runs forever. That is an infinite loop — and in a hospital ventilator or a car's braking system, it can be lethal.'
Say: 'A FOR loop is predictable — it always stops. A WHILE loop is flexible — but it only stops if the condition can actually change. If it cannot, you have a disaster.'
Ask: 'Who is responsible for making sure a WHILE-loop stop condition is reachable? The programmer? The sensor manufacturer? The hospital?'
lessonSlides.realWorldConnection: The Boeing 737 MAX crashes involved feedback loops in the MCAS system. The system repeatedly pushed the nose down based on sensor input. The sensor was faulty, but the loop kept running — pilots could not override it fast enough. The loop logic worked as designed. The design was unsafe. 346 people died.
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If FOR vs WHILE feels abstract, try: 'A FOR loop is like a washing machine cycle — 30 minutes, then it stops. A WHILE loop is like a smoke alarm — it keeps checking while smoke is detected. What happens if the smoke detector breaks and always detects smoke?'
lessonSlides.roleModelMoment: Margaret Hamilton's Apollo guidance computer used loop priority logic to prevent overload during flight. Choosing the right loop type was not academic — it was a live safety requirement.

lessonSlides.title: FOR vs WHILE Decisions
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Classify each scenario: is it a FOR loop or a WHILE loop? For each WHILE loop, name the stop condition — and predict what happens if it breaks.'
whatToDo: This exercise runs during Phase 3.
Push students to always name the stop condition for WHILE loops.
If students classify everything as FOR, challenge: 'Can you use a FOR loop for a heater? How many times should it run? You do not know — that is why it needs WHILE.'

lessonSlides.title: Climate Data — FOR Loop
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Now you are going to write a real loop. Look at the 10 readings on the board. Your task: write a FOR loop that checks every reading and flags a WARNING if it is above 3 degrees.'
whatToDo: Point to the dataset: −2, 4, 1, −5, 8, 3.2, 0, 6, −1, 5
Say: 'Write this in plain language — not code. FOR each reading in the dataset: IF reading > 3, print WARNING. OTHERWISE print SAFE.'
Give students 2 minutes to trace through all 10 readings on paper.
Expected results:
−2 → SAFE, 4 → WARNING, 1 → SAFE, −5 → SAFE, 8 → WARNING, 3.2 → WARNING, 0 → SAFE, 6 → WARNING, −1 → SAFE, 5 → WARNING
Ask: 'How many warnings?' Answer: 5 (readings: 4, 8, 3.2, 6, 5).
Close: 'You just processed 10 readings with one instruction. Climate scientists use the same logic on 3.5 billion readings. Same loop — bigger array.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If students make errors tracing, slow down: 'Go reading by reading. −2: is it above 3? No. SAFE. 4: is it above 3? Yes. WARNING. Keep going — the loop works the same way every time.'

lessonSlides.title: Early Stop and Averaging
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Your monitoring system does not need to process all 10 readings. It needs to raise an alert after three warnings. Modify your loop to stop after three warnings — and compute the average of those three flagged readings.'
whatToDo: Form groups of three.
Say: 'You need two new things: a counter that tracks how many warnings you have found, and a running total to calculate the average.'
Walk around. Key questions to ask groups:
'Where does your loop store the warning count? It needs a variable.'
'When do you stop? After the counter reaches 3.'
'What type of loop is this now? You started with FOR — but now you need to stop early based on a condition. Is that still a FOR loop?'
Guide: 'Strictly, this is now a WHILE loop — WHILE warning count < 3 AND there are readings left. But many languages let you break out of a FOR loop early.'
Expected first three warnings: reading 4 (index 2), reading 8 (index 5), reading 3.2 (index 6). Total: 15.2. Average: 15.2 ÷ 3 ≈ 5.07°C.
After most groups finish, verify the answer together: 'The first three flagged readings are 4, 8, and 3.2. Average: about 5.07 degrees above normal.'
Close: 'Margaret Hamilton's Apollo software did exactly this — it stopped non-essential tasks when the computer was overloaded. That is an early-stop condition. You just built one.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If groups forget the counter, push: 'Your loop has no memory right now. It checks readings but does not remember how many warnings it has found. How does it know when to stop? You need a variable — a piece of information the loop carries with it.'
lessonSlides.roleModelMoment: Hamilton's early-exit condition during Apollo 11 worked on exactly this principle: stop lower-priority processing when something critical needs the CPU. Your early-stop loop encodes the same decision.

lessonSlides.title: Climate Data Parsing Loop
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Write a FOR loop to flag readings above 3°C. Then modify it: stop after three warnings and compute their average.'
rules: Write in plain language — not code syntax.
Trace every reading and mark WARNING or SAFE.
Add a warning counter and a running total.
Compute and verify the average: approximately 5.07°C.
whatToDo: This exercise spans Phase 4 Rounds 1 and 2.
See the Phase 4 slides for full facilitation instructions.

lessonSlides.title: Discussion
whatToDo: Pick the question below that feels most alive based on how the class went.
One strong discussion is better than two rushed ones.
Give students a moment to think before taking answers.
discussionQuestions: 'Your loop uses a threshold of 3 degrees. Who decided that number? What if the ocean baseline shifts — should the threshold change? Who authorises that change, and how often should it be reviewed?'

'In critical infrastructure — hospitals, aircraft, power grids — WHILE loops monitor sensor data continuously. Who is responsible if the stop condition becomes unreachable? The programmer? The sensor manufacturer? The operator?'
'The Boeing 737 MAX had a feedback loop that worked as designed — but the design was unsafe. 346 people died. Is the loop the problem, or the people who approved the design?'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If discussion is slow, try: 'You set a threshold of 3 degrees for today. But what if ocean temperatures rise so much that 3 degrees above average becomes the new normal? Your loop would flag every single reading. Is the threshold wrong — or has the planet changed? Who updates the number?'

lessonSlides.title: Exit Reflection — Choosing Loop Types
lessonSlides.whatToSay: 'Final task. On your own, complete this sentence: A FOR loop is better when ______, and a WHILE loop is better when ______.'

lessonSlides.title: Close the Lesson
whatToDo: Ask every student to write and complete the sentence on paper or sticky note.
They must use one concrete classroom example for each blank.
They must check that their WHILE example has a reachable stop condition.
Collect papers or stick them on the board.
Read 2–3 strong responses aloud.
lessonSlides.exitReflection: A FOR loop is better when ______, and a WHILE loop is better when ______.
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Close with: 'Margaret Hamilton proved that loops are not just about saving time — they are about saving lives. You wrote loop logic for climate data today. She wrote it for the Moon landing. The principle is the same: repeat carefully, stop safely, and know what happens if the data changes. Next lesson, you will learn how large teams build software by breaking problems into independent parts — functions and modules.'
lessonSlides.conversationStarter: If reflections are shallow, push: 'Do not just say FOR is for counting and WHILE is for conditions. Use your examples. FOR checking 10 climate readings — you know there are 10. WHILE heating a room — you do not know when it reaches 20 degrees. Be concrete.'
